video_title: Lawfare Daily: AI Targeting Systems Are Coming—But Not as Fast as You Think
gist: Carnegie's Steve Feldstein argues that despite the Pentagon's Maven-driven strike tempo in the Iran war, full AI targeting systems require decades of deep infrastructure (ISR, compute, trained algorithms) and won't diffuse to other militaries quickly like software does; instead, capital-rich, threat-exposed states like the UAE are the most likely near-term adopters, while chip and normative pressure -- not model export controls -- are the only realistic levers to slow proliferation.

themes.theme: What building an AI targeting system actually requires

insights.claim: During the 38-day Iran war, the Pentagon's use of Palantir's Maven surged over 4,000% to roughly 20 billion tokens a day, with 13,000 targets struck, but this reflects a uniquely mature US infrastructure rather than a capability any military can now replicate.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein (Carnegie Endowment)
insights.so_what: Headline strike-tempo numbers from the Iran war overstate how close other militaries are to matching US AI-targeting capability -- useful counterweight to "AI warfare is proliferating" narratives.
insights.quote: the Pentagon's use of Maven surged to something like what 20 billion tokens a day and it was like over 4,000% increase and 13,000 targets struck

insights.claim: Palantir's Maven is a user-interface layer that aggregates disparate intelligence streams into a click-through workflow from target identification to strike authorization, rather than a standalone AI model comparable to a chatbot like Claude.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating Palantir's defense-software moat should understand Maven's value is in orchestration/UX over existing data pipelines, not in a proprietary foundation model.
insights.quote: Palunteer's Maven is essentially a user interface software packaging that helps to bring together different streams of data to facilitate decision-m

insights.claim: A working AI targeting system requires a deep, multi-decade intelligence-surveillance-reconnaissance (ISR) apparatus combining real-time satellite imagery, signals intelligence, phone intercepts, and social media (e.g., Telegram in the Ukraine war) before any algorithm can be usefully trained on it.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: This is the real barrier to entry for AI-targeting diffusion -- it favors incumbents with pre-existing surveillance infrastructure over countries simply buying software off the shelf.

insights.claim: Little vetting occurs on the raw intelligence fed into targeting algorithms -- the operating premise, dating back to Palantir's founding in 2003 for post-9/11 counterterrorism, is that humans cannot sift the data volume so the system is trusted to filter noise from signal.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Raises an accuracy/liability risk investors and policymakers should weight when assessing targeting-software vendors: the system's core value proposition (handling unvettable data volume) is also its core error source.
insights.quote: essentially you're put inputting a bunch of noise and then you're hoping the system will be able to kind of pierce through the noise and derive insights

insights.claim: US military computer-vision targeting models that achieved roughly 70% success rates in dusty, desert-like theaters such as Afghanistan fell to under 30% when applied to dense-rainforest terrain like the Philippines, showing target-recognition accuracy is highly terrain- and context-dependent.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: A concrete, quotable data point on why AI targeting can't simply be exported turnkey to a new theater -- retraining for local terrain is a real cost and time constraint on diffusion speed.

themes.theme: Compute and cloud infrastructure as the hidden bottleneck

insights.claim: Israel's Unit 8200 built a surveillance capacity intercepting roughly a million phone calls a day from Palestinians in the West Bank and Gaza but ran out of internal server capacity, forcing it to contract Microsoft to store and process the data on Azure servers based in the Netherlands and Ireland.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Even a top-tier military/intelligence service is compute-constrained and dependent on US hyperscaler capacity, underscoring how much of the modern kill chain runs through commercial cloud infrastructure rather than sovereign systems.
insights.quote: it soon ran out of space on his servers uh internally. So right so it didn't have the data.

insights.claim: Project Nimbus, the 2021 cloud-computing contract between Google, AWS, and the Israeli Defense Forces, is worth $1.2 billion and supplies machine-learning tools and storage/infrastructure capacity that most countries lack the resources to match.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: A concrete revenue data point on defense-cloud contracts and evidence that the compute/storage layer -- not the AI model -- is the scarce, capital-intensive chokepoint in military AI, favoring the largest hyperscalers.
insights.quote: I think project Nimbus like this cloud computing contract that was signed between Google, a AWS and the IDF, the Israeli uh defense forces in 2021 is a $1.2 billion contract.

insights.claim: Microsoft withdrew from an Azure-related contract tied to Israeli military targeting operations after reputational pressure from a Guardian investigation, suggesting cloud providers view themselves as bearing some culpability in the kill chain despite not directly authorizing strikes.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Signals reputational/ESG risk for hyperscalers supplying military AI infrastructure -- a factor investors in Microsoft, Google, or Amazon should track as press scrutiny of defense-cloud contracts grows.
insights.quote: when called out upon it reputationally by a guardian investigation they have pulled out from the contract

themes.theme: Accountability is diffused across a 'stack,' not a breakable chain

insights.claim: The modern military kill chain is better understood as a diffuse web or stack of interdependent private and state actors (cloud providers, software interface makers, drone/munitions manufacturers) rather than a linear chain with a single choke point that can be broken to stop a strike.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Policy or activist efforts aimed at a single vendor (e.g., pressuring one cloud provider to exit a contract) are unlikely to meaningfully disrupt AI-enabled targeting because redundant commercial suppliers exist across the stack.
insights.quote: there really isn't a chain. It's kind of a web or it's kind of a stack and the stack is diffuse and has many options on how it can go.

insights.claim: Legal and military accountability for AI-assisted strikes ultimately rests with the Pentagon as the 'owner' of the kill chain, even though private vendors like Palantir, Microsoft, and Google supply the software and infrastructure that shape decision-making.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: For regulatory or litigation risk assessments, the accountable party in AI-targeting harms is the commissioning military, not the software/cloud vendor -- relevant to how liability risk should be priced for defense-tech suppliers.
insights.quote: there is an owner of the kill chain, that's the Pentagon.

insights.claim: Palantir's Maven interface, which compresses the targeting process into roughly four clicks, sits closer to the 'end product' of the kill chain than the underlying data infrastructure, making it a more direct point of decision-bias and accountability than cloud or surveillance providers further upstream.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Differentiates Palantir's exposure/accountability profile from that of pure infrastructure providers like Microsoft or Google in any future regulatory or reputational scrutiny of military AI.
insights.quote: that interface that puts it all together, that compresses the killchain, that gives you four clicks towards uh destroying a target, that's probably closer in line ... to the end product

themes.theme: Diffusion economics: nuclear-weapon pace, not chatbot pace

insights.claim: AI targeting systems are more analogous to nuclear weapons diffusion -- slow, capital-intensive, closed, with costs not commensurate with most countries' benefit -- than to consumer AI diffusion, where an innovation like ChatGPT went from a handful of developers to global mass adoption within a couple of years.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers should not extrapolate the rapid global diffusion curve of consumer AI onto military AI targeting; the relevant comparison for pacing expectations is nuclear proliferation, which argues against near-term widespread military AI adoption.
insights.quote: the converse of that is the spread of something like open AI's you know uh like chatbot like chat GPT ... that's an example of rapid democratized diffusion across the globe

insights.claim: The UAE is the most likely next adopter of full AI targeting capability because it combines political incentive (a volatile neighborhood and unpredictable adversaries), pre-existing surveillance infrastructure, extensive AI data-center investment, and a domestic military-industrial push (its EDGE conglomerate) to build homegrown drones and an arms industry.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: A specific, actionable forecast for defense-tech and geopolitical-risk watchers on which state is next in line for AI-targeting capability -- relevant to Gulf defense-spending and AI-infrastructure themes.

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia, India, and Brazil are named as the next tier of 'rising powers' with sufficient capacity to plausibly adopt AI targeting systems in the coming years, behind the UAE.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Extends the watchlist of states for defense-AI-adoption tracking beyond the primary UAE call, useful for scenario planning around future defense-AI export/contract flows.

themes.theme: Drones vs. targeting systems: two very different diffusion curves

insights.claim: Combat drones diffused far faster than AI targeting systems will -- from $30 million exquisite US Reaper drones used for signature strikes, to Turkey's low-cost TB2 medium-range drones, to Ukraine's 2022-era mass adoption of $500 consumer quadcopters retrofitted with munitions -- because they required comparatively little specialized infrastructure to replicate at scale.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Explains why cheap hardware-centric military technologies (drones) proliferate on a completely different timeline than data/infrastructure-heavy AI targeting systems -- a useful mental model for forecasting which defense-AI segments commoditize fastest.
insights.quote: a very simple quadcopter that you could buy for $500 off of Amazon, you could outfit it with a basic munition and create tremendous damage

insights.claim: Full comprehensive AI targeting systems like Maven will likely remain limited to a small group of high-capacity militaries for the near future, while the nearer-term proliferation risk is 'last-mile' autonomous weapons -- e.g., drones already deployed in Ukraine that use onboard computer vision to lock onto targets after losing contact with their human operator.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Redirects near-term proliferation/regulatory attention away from Maven-style comprehensive systems and toward edge-device autonomous targeting, where oversight gaps are emerging first.

themes.theme: Policy levers: chips and norms, not model export controls

insights.claim: Export controls on AI software and models are not a realistic chokepoint for slowing military AI diffusion because 'the cat's out of the bag' -- weights, distilled models, and APIs are effectively globally accessible already.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Pushes back on a policy consensus favoring model-level export controls; investors and policymakers evaluating AI-safety/export-control proposals should weight chip and infrastructure controls far more heavily than software restrictions.
insights.quote: I think that at least on software and model I think the cat's out of the bat. I don't really think there's much we can do on that front.

insights.claim: Restricting the flow of cutting-edge chips and data-center/surveillance infrastructure can't stop major militaries from acquiring AI targeting capability but can meaningfully slow second- and third-tier state actors who lack the capital to route around such controls.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Suggests chip export-control policy (e.g., US restrictions relevant to Nvidia and similar suppliers) has real teeth against smaller/poorer state actors even if ineffective against top-tier militaries -- a nuance for assessing chip-export-policy efficacy.
insights.quote: you won't stop major militaries, but you can stop second and third tier actors maybe from acquiring as easily as they would like these types of uh these types of systems

insights.claim: Normative and public political pressure can raise the effective cost of AI-targeting acquisition for states -- turning it into a resource trade-off where leaders weigh, e.g., spending $150 million on a system against the political price of pursuing it -- even absent binding treaties.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Identifies reputational/political pressure campaigns (media investigations, activism) as a genuine, if soft, lever on AI-targeting proliferation -- relevant to how quickly demand for these systems might grow among mid-tier states.
insights.quote: the more you can start to to create a political cost for countries that are thinking about getting them, the more that uh it becomes a resource trade-off question

themes.theme: Human oversight is becoming a legal fiction

insights.claim: Meaningful human oversight of AI-assisted targeting is already eroding for the most sophisticated militaries -- reviews compressed to as little as seven seconds do not constitute genuine human control compared to the hours-long, multi-step vetting process (analyst, lawyer, commander sign-off) that used to precede a strike.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Undercuts the policy assumption that 'human in the loop' requirements provide real safety guardrails as targeting cycles compress from hours to minutes -- relevant to how much weight regulators/investors should give HITL compliance claims.
insights.quote: I don't know if 7-second reviews count as meaningful humanight when it comes to the targeting cycle that we've described with with Maven.

insights.claim: International humanitarian law frameworks have not kept pace with AI-enabled targeting speed, leaving a growing accountability gap as systems like Lavender generate kill lists near-automatically and Maven compresses detection-to-strike into minutes.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Flags an unresolved legal/regulatory risk area that could eventually produce new binding rules or litigation exposure for both militaries and the private vendors supplying targeting software.

insights.claim: A prevailing but contestable theory driving investment in ever-faster AI targeting is that speed and scale determine who wins modern wars, such that sacrificing pace for greater human accountability is framed as a path to losing -- a framing Feldstein calls a partly false choice.
insights.speaker: guest, Steve Feldstein
insights.so_what: Identifies the core strategic justification driving defense-AI capex (speed-wins-wars doctrine) that investors should recognize as a debated assumption, not settled fact, when evaluating demand durability for targeting software.
insights.quote: I think that's a little bit of a false choice, but I I don't think you can dismiss those those arguments either.
